memes.label: "The Painted Veil"
memes.description: "The Painted Veil — The Painted Veil by W. Somerset Maugham"

memes.label: "Uncopyright"
memes.description: "Uncopyright — The Painted Veil by W. Somerset Maugham"

memes.label: Adultery
memes.description: Kitty Fane's extramarital affair with Charles Townsend — the central transgression that sets the novel's plot in motion and drives her moral journey.

memes.label: Affection
memes.description: A feeling of fondness and emotional warmth that in The Painted Veil occupies a moral middle ground — warmer than duty but cooler than love, capable of sustaining relationships that passion cannot, and often the truest emotion characters are capable of offering one another.

memes.label: Ambition
memes.description: The driving social force embodied primarily by Mrs. Garstin in The Painted Veil, whose relentless ambition to advance her family's status shapes the fates of her daughters and ultimately reveals the hollow nature of social striving.

memes.label: Appearance and Reality
memes.description: "The central thematic tension of The Painted Veil — the gap between surface appearances and underlying truth, between social facade and inner moral reality."

memes.label: Beauty
memes.description: Physical beauty as a form of social currency in The Painted Veil — Kitty's greatest asset and ultimate liability, valued by the world but revealed as hollow by the novel's moral standards.

memes.label: Bitterness
memes.description: The corrosive emotional residue of unresolved injury — Walter Fane's bitterness at Kitty's betrayal, and Kitty's own bitterness at the social system that has shaped her, bitterness in The Painted Veil is poison that must be metabolized or it destroys.

memes.label: The British Class System
memes.description: The social hierarchy of Edwardian England — the invisible institution that governs marriage, profession, and identity for all the novel's British characters.

memes.label: Buddhist Monastery
memes.description: A Buddhist monastery situated ten miles from Mei-tan-fu, visited by Kitty and Waddington on a Sunday excursion (053-001).

memes.label: Character Index
memes.description: Index of all character pages in The Painted Veil wiki, dynamically rendered from the person-type entries

memes.label: Charity
memes.description: The active practice of selfless love and service embodied by the French Catholic nuns in The Painted Veil, representing an alternative moral order to the novel's world of social ambition and romantic passion.

memes.label: Charles Townsend
memes.description: The Assistant Colonial Secretary in Hong Kong — charming, vain, and morally hollow, Charles Townsend is the object of Kitty's adulterous passion and the novel's definitive portrait of masculine egotism disguised as sophistication.

memes.label: Cholera
memes.description: The cholera epidemic raging in Mei-tan-fu — the physical and symbolic crucible that strips away pretense and forces the novel's characters to confront mortality, duty, and meaning.

memes.label: The Cholera Epidemic
memes.description: The cholera outbreak that ravages Mei-tan-fu — the novel's great leveler, creating the conditions in which Walter's competence shines, Kitty's character is tested, and the nuns' compassion finds its fullest expression.

memes.label: Colonel Yü
memes.description: The Chinese military commander in Mei-tan-fu — a figure of quiet authority who represents the Chinese governance that exists parallel to and independent of the British colonial presence.

memes.label: Colonial Government (Hong Kong)
memes.description: The British colonial administration in Hong Kong — the institutional framework within which the novel's characters live, work, and pursue or fail to pursue their ambitions.

memes.label: Compassion
memes.description: The capacity for feeling another's suffering as one's own — embodied by the French nuns and gradually awakened in Kitty through her exposure to the cholera epidemic, compassion represents the novel's highest moral value.

memes.label: Composure
memes.description: Composure in The Painted Veil is a surface of calm restraint that conceals intense emotion — a British colonial virtue that the novel both admires and critiques.

memes.label: Concept & Theme Index
memes.description: Index of all concept and theme pages in The Painted Veil wiki, dynamically rendered from the concept-type entries

memes.label: Contempt
memes.description: A pervasive moral-emotional force in The Painted Veil, expressed through Walter Fane's cold disdain for Kitty's infidelity, colonial social hierarchies, and Kitty's self-contempt for her own weakness.

memes.label: Convent
memes.description: The French Catholic convent in cholera-stricken Mei-tan-fu — a sanctuary of faith, service, and feminine community that becomes the crucible of Kitty Fane's moral and spiritual transformation.

memes.label: Courage
memes.description: The various forms of courage displayed by characters in The Painted Veil — physical, moral, and spiritual — that define the novel's spectrum of human response to suffering.

memes.label: Cruelty
memes.description: A recurring moral theme in The Painted Veil — cruelty manifests through social manipulation, emotional withdrawal, and colonial indifference.

memes.label: Death
memes.description: The omnipresent reality of death in The Painted Veil — the cholera epidemic that serves as the novel's moral crucible, and Walter Fane's death as the catalytic event that propels Kitty toward self-knowledge.

memes.label: Desire
memes.description: The powerful force of physical and emotional wanting that drives the central transgression of The Painted Veil — Kitty's affair with Charles Townsend — and recurs throughout the novel as a counterpoint to duty, restraint, and spiritual aspiration.

memes.label: Dignity
memes.description: "The quality of bearing that commands respect without demanding it — in The Painted Veil, dignity is the unspoken moral currency of the colonial world: possessed absolutely by Mother Superior, performed strategically by Walter, and gradually learned by Kitty as she discovers that dignity is not inherited but earned through suffering."

memes.label: Doris Dennison
memes.description: Kitty's younger sister, whose early marriage to Geoffrey Dennison precipitates Kitty's panic-driven acceptance of Walter Fane's proposal — the catalyst for the novel's entire plot.

memes.label: Doris Garstin
memes.description: Doris Garstin is Kitty Fane's younger sister in The Painted Veil, whose conventional success in the marriage market precipitates Kitty's panic marriage to Walter Fane and whose engagement to a baronet's son crystallises the Edwardian social pressures that drive the novel's opening chapters.

memes.label: Doris's Engagement
memes.description: The announcement of Doris Garstin's engagement to Geoffrey Dennison — the social event that precipitates Kitty's panic and her fateful acceptance of Walter Fane's proposal.

memes.label: Dorothy Townsend
memes.description: Charles Townsend's wife — a competent, pleasant woman whose quiet dignity contrasts sharply with her husband's moral emptiness, Dorothy represents the domestic stability that Townsend exploits but will never sacrifice.

memes.label: Dr. Watson
memes.description: The missionary doctor who preceded Walter Fane in Mei-tan-fu — already dead of cholera when the Fanes arrive, Watson haunts the novel as an absent standard against which Walter is measured.

memes.label: Duty
memes.description: The moral framework of obligation and responsibility that governs Walter Fane's actions and, ultimately, becomes the ethical standard against which all characters are measured in The Painted Veil.

memes.label: Escape
memes.description: The recurring pattern in The Painted Veil of Kitty Fane's attempts to flee constraining circumstances — her mother's house, her loveless marriage, and the cholera-stricken Mei-tan-fu — and her ultimate discovery that true escape is internal rather than geographical.

memes.label: Event Index
memes.description: Index of all event pages in The Painted Veil wiki, dynamically rendered from the event-type entries

memes.label: Faith
memes.description: Religious faith as embodied by the French Catholic nuns in Mei-tan-fu — a spiritual framework that stands in quiet contrast to the secular, socially-bound world of the British colonial characters.

memes.label: The Fanes' Bungalow
memes.description: The Fanes' modest home in Hong Kong's Pleasant Vale — the domestic stage for Kitty's discontent, her adulterous passion, and Walter's devastating discovery.

memes.label: Fear
memes.description: Fear in The Painted Veil operates as both a psychological state and a narrative force — the fear of scandal, of disease, of self-knowledge — that strips away characters' pretensions and reveals their essential natures.

memes.label: Forgiveness
memes.description: The moral act of releasing resentment — central to the novel's resolution as Kitty seeks Walter's forgiveness for her betrayal and, ultimately, her own self-forgiveness for the person she has been.

memes.label: Freedom
memes.description: The state of self-determination that Kitty gradually achieves — freedom from her mother's expectations, from Walter's quiet tyranny, from Townsend's charm, and ultimately from her own vanity, freedom in the novel is hard-won and internally defined.

memes.label: Geoffrey Dennison
memes.description: Doris Garstin's husband, a young man destined to inherit a baronetcy — his engagement to Doris precipitates Kitty's crisis and, by extension, the novel's entire plot.

memes.label: Government House
memes.description: The seat of British colonial authority in Hong Kong — a symbol of the social hierarchy that determines the characters' status and the pinnacle of the world Kitty aspires to but can never fully enter.

memes.label: Grief
memes.description: The intense sorrow and mourning that characters experience in response to loss — most notably Kitty Fane's complex, often suppressed grief following Walter's death, and the performative grief of Doris Garstin at their mother's funeral.

memes.label: Happiness
memes.description: An elusive state in The Painted Veil — happiness is pursued, fleetingly attained, and ultimately redefined through suffering and spiritual growth.

memes.label: Hong Kong
memes.description: The British crown colony where the novel's first half unfolds — a world of social ambition, colonial administration, and the stifling conventions that Kitty Fane must escape to discover herself.

memes.label: Honour
memes.description: Honour in The Painted Veil is intertwined with duty, reputation, and unselfishness — a social and moral code that characters either uphold or betray.

memes.label: Humiliation
memes.description: The experience of being lowered in dignity or self-respect — a pervasive emotional condition in The Painted Veil that afflicts characters across the social spectrum, from Kitty's sexual shame to Walter's cuckolded pride to Mrs. Garstin's social striving.

memes.label: Illusion
memes.description: The central metaphor of The Painted Veil — the deceptive surface of life that conceals deeper truths, drawn from Shelley's sonnet and woven through every major character's arc of self-discovery and disillusionment.

memes.label: Indifference
memes.description: The absence of emotional engagement — a state that characters in The Painted Veil alternately suffer, cultivate as self-protection, and weaponise against others, from Walter's icy composure to Kitty's eventual liberation from passion.

memes.label: Journey to Mei-tan-fu
memes.description: The grueling overland journey from Hong Kong to the cholera-stricken interior — a physical ordeal that marks the transition from the novel's social world to its moral one.

memes.label: Kindness
memes.description: The quality of being gentle, considerate, and generous toward others — a virtues that appears in The Painted Veil in guises ranging from genuine compassion (the nuns at Mei-tan-fu) to strategic manipulation (Charles Townsend's charm), and whose unexpected appearance often proves the most emotionally devastating.

memes.label: Kitty Fane
memes.description: Kitty Fane (née Garstin) is the protagonist of W. Somerset Maugham's The Painted Veil, a young Englishwoman whose journey from frivolous socialite to self-aware woman in cholera-stricken Mei-tan-fu forms the novel's central arc of moral and spiritual awakening.

memes.label: Kitty's Affair with Townsend
memes.description: Kitty Fane's adulterous affair with Charles Townsend — the inciting crisis of the novel, a relationship built on romantic self-deception that, when exposed, sets the entire plot in motion.

memes.label: Kitty's Work at the Convent
memes.description: Kitty's growing involvement with the French convent in Mei-tan-fu — caring for orphans, assisting the nuns, and discovering a sense of purpose independent of male approval.

memes.label: Kitty's Departure from Mei-tan-fu
memes.description: Kitty's departure from the city where she was transformed — leaving behind Walter's grave, the convent, and the woman she used to be.

memes.label: Kitty's Final Resolution
memes.description: Kitty's decision to accompany her father to his new post in the Bahamas and to raise her child to be independent and fearless — the novel's concluding statement on whether genuine change is possible.

memes.label: Kitty's London Seasons
memes.description: Kitty's years in the London marriage market — seasons of dazzling social success that paradoxically failed to produce the brilliant marriage her mother demanded, creating the conditions for her eventual acceptance of Walter.

memes.label: Kitty's Return to Hong Kong
memes.description: Kitty's return to Hong Kong after Walter's death, and her devastating relapse with Townsend, testing whether her transformation in Mei-tan-fu was genuine.

memes.label: London
memes.description: The social world of Edwardian London that produced Kitty Garstin and the values she must overcome — a marriage market governed by ambition, appearance, and the relentless pressure of social expectation.

memes.label: Love
memes.description: The central emotional force in The Painted Veil — explored through romantic passion, marital estrangement, maternal tenderness, and spiritual compassion as characters navigate the gulf between idealized love and its flawed human expression.

memes.label: Love vs. Convention
memes.description: A central thematic tension in The Painted Veil — romantic love and sexual passion contend against social convention, familial duty, and colonial morality.

memes.label: The Manchu Woman
memes.description: Waddington's companion in Mei-tan-fu — a Manchu woman of aristocratic origin whose quiet dignity and unassimilated presence challenge British colonial assumptions about race, class, and domesticity.

memes.label: Marriage
memes.description: The social institution of marriage as a central thematic concern in The Painted Veil — a framework for examining duty, social ambition, and personal fulfillment in early 20th-century British society.

memes.label: Mei-tan-fu
memes.description: The remote Chinese city devastated by cholera where Kitty Fane undergoes her moral transformation — a place of death and beauty that functions as the novel's spiritual crucible.

memes.label: Mother
memes.description: The role of motherhood as portrayed through Mrs. Garstin's calculating ambition, Kitty's unexpected pregnancy, and the maternal compassion modeled by the French nuns — a spectrum from social coercion to selfless care.

memes.label: Mother Superior
memes.description: The French Catholic Mother Superior of the convent in Mei-tan-fu — a figure of genuine spiritual authority whose practical compassion and unpretentious wisdom provide the novel's moral compass.

memes.label: Mr. Garstin
memes.description: Kitty's father, a mild-mannered barrister whose professional disappointments and emotional withdrawal from his family provide the novel's quietest portrait of a life diminished by duty without love.

memes.label: Mrs. Garstin
memes.description: Kitty's ambitious, calculating mother whose social-climbing aspirations shape her daughters' destinies — a portrait of maternal love distorted into strategic investment.

memes.label: Mrs. Garstin's Death
memes.description: The death of Kitty's mother, reported near the novel's end, removing the primary source of the social pressure that shaped Kitty's life and opening the possibility of a different relationship with her father.

memes.label: Organization Index
memes.description: Index of all organization pages in The Painted Veil wiki, dynamically rendered from the organization-type entries

memes.label: Passion
memes.description: Intense, often overwhelming erotic and emotional desire — the force that drives Kitty Fane into her affair with Charles Townsend, that Walter Fane conceals beneath his cold exterior, and that the novel interrogates as both life-giving and destructive.

memes.label: Place Index
memes.description: Index of all location pages in The Painted Veil wiki, dynamically rendered from the place-type entries

memes.label: Pleasure
memes.description: "The experience of enjoyment and sensory satisfaction — in The Painted Veil, pleasure is a moral fault line: sought by Kitty as escape from emptiness, offered by Charles Townsend as seduction, and ultimately recognised as insufficient to fill the void it temporarily distracts from."

memes.label: Pride
memes.description: The assertion of self-worth that in The Painted Veil takes both destructive forms — Walter's cold pride, Kitty's social pride — and, potentially, redemptive forms as Kitty begins to take legitimate pride in her work and moral growth.

memes.label: Redemption
memes.description: Kitty Fane's journey from moral transgression through suffering to spiritual redemption, the central character arc of The Painted Veil.

memes.label: Regret
memes.description: The profound and transformative experience of remorse in The Painted Veil, particularly Kitty Fane's bitter regret over her adulterous affair and its consequences for Walter, herself, and her understanding of love.

memes.label: Resentment
memes.description: A slow-burning anger born of perceived unfairness — in The Painted Veil, resentment is the emotional groundwater that seeps through marriages, families, and colonial hierarchies, poisoning relationships that survive on the surface but rot from within.

memes.label: Sacrifice
memes.description: The giving up of something valued for the sake of something else — in The Painted Veil, sacrifice appears in Walter's professional devotion, the nuns' renunciation of worldly life, and Kitty's gradual relinquishment of her former self.

memes.label: Science
memes.description: The domain of rational inquiry and professional expertise embodied by Walter Fane as a bacteriologist — positioned in the novel as both a contrast to emotional life and a source of genuine human value through the cholera epidemic response.

memes.label: Self-Respect
memes.description: The internal conviction of one's own worth — in The Painted Veil, self-respect is what Kitty Fane has never possessed and spends the entire novel struggling to acquire, a quality that must be built from the rubble of humiliation rather than inherited from social position.

memes.label: Shame
memes.description: The painful self-consciousness of moral failure — experienced by Kitty through her adultery, her social humiliation, and her dawning recognition of her own moral inadequacy, shame functions as both punishment and catalyst for change.

memes.label: Sin
memes.description: The moral-religious framework through which the novel examines Kitty's adultery, Walter's unforgiving rage, and the broader question of whether human failings demand punishment, forgiveness, or something beyond both.

memes.label: Sister St. Joseph
memes.description: A French nun at the Mei-tan-fu convent — cheerful, practical, and warmly human, Sister St. Joseph embodies religious devotion without the solemnity that might intimidate the secular Kitty.

memes.label: Social Class
memes.description: The rigid social hierarchy of British colonial society that shapes the ambitions, constraints, and moral compromises of the Garstin family in The Painted Veil.

memes.label: Standard Ebooks
memes.description: The digital publisher of the edition used for this wiki's source text — a volunteer-driven project producing free, high-quality digital editions of public domain literature.

memes.label: Tea at the Convent
memes.description: The ritual of afternoon tea at the Convent in Mei-tan-fu marks Kitty's entry into a world governed not by colonial social convention but by spiritual discipline — a threshold moment in her moral transformation.

memes.label: Tenderness
memes.description: The quality of gentle affection that Walter Fane reveals only in fragments throughout The Painted Veil — a hidden tenderness that makes his emotional isolation and eventual death tragically poignant, and that Kitty recognizes too late.

memes.label: The Amah
memes.description: Kitty's Chinese maid in Hong Kong and Mei-tan-fu — a peripheral but constant presence whose quiet observation of Kitty's crises represents the Chinese perspective that the British characters rarely consider.

memes.label: The Government Bacteriological Service
memes.description: The specialized medical institution that employs Walter Fane — a professional organization devoted to public health in the colonies, representing the scientific expertise that colonial society undervalues.

memes.label: British Army Garrison
memes.description: The British military presence in Hong Kong and Mei-tan-fu — a background institution representing imperial power and the security that enables colonial administration.

memes.label: The Causeway
memes.description: The narrow raised path through the rice-fields of southern China that serves as the physical and symbolic artery of Kitty Fane's journey to and from Mei-tan-fu.

memes.label: The Cemetery
memes.description: The burial ground in Mei-tan-fu where cholera victims and Walter Fane are interred — a place where death, the novel's great equalizer, strips away all social pretense.

memes.label: The Chinese Administration in Mei-tan-fu
memes.description: The local Chinese governmental structure in Mei-tan-fu that exists parallel to British colonial authority — a reminder that the British are not the only, or primary, actors in the region.

memes.label: The Chinese Customs
memes.description: The Chinese Customs administration in The Painted Veil represents the institutional interface between British colonial authority and Chinese governance — a bureaucratic structure that employs characters like Waddington and embodies the practical complexities of colonial rule.

memes.label: The British Civil Service
memes.description: The professional bureaucracy that employs Walter Fane and Charles Townsend — an institution that rewards different qualities in different contexts, valuing Townsend's charm in Hong Kong and Walter's competence in a cholera zone.

memes.label: The Club
memes.description: The Hong Kong Club — the all-male social institution where colonial officials gather, gossip, and consolidate the power that Kitty, as a woman, can only access indirectly.

memes.label: The Club
memes.description: The Hong Kong Club as a social institution — the all-male bastion of colonial networking where careers are made, reputations forged, and women excluded from direct access to power.

memes.label: The Convent
memes.description: The French Catholic convent in Mei-tan-fu — a sanctuary of compassion and order amid the cholera epidemic, and the place where Kitty Fane discovers an alternative to the social values that have governed her life.

memes.label: The Convent Infirmary
memes.description: The medical facility within the Mei-tan-fu convent — an improvised organization of care where French nuns and Chinese patients meet in the common experience of suffering and service.

memes.label: The Convent
memes.description: The French Catholic convent in Mei-tan-fu as an institution — a community of nuns devoted to the care of orphans and the sick, representing organized compassion as an alternative to colonial self-interest.

memes.label: The Curio Shop
memes.description: The Hong Kong curio shop where Kitty and Townsend conduct their clandestine meetings — a place of rented anonymity that reflects the transaction-like nature of their affair.

memes.label: The Customs House
memes.description: The point of arrival in Mei-tan-fu — the threshold where Kitty passes from the familiar colonial world into the cholera-stricken city that will transform her.

memes.label: The Dinner Party
memes.description: Dinner parties in The Painted Veil function as the primary arena of colonial social performance — the ritualised gatherings where status is negotiated, attractions are kindled, and the gap between public propriety and private desire is most visible.

memes.label: The French Catholic Mission
memes.description: The broader French Catholic missionary organization of which the Mei-tan-fu convent is one outpost — an international religious institution that provides the institutional context for the nuns' work.

memes.label: The Garstin Household
memes.description: The domestic institution of the Garstin family — a household organized around Mrs. Garstin's ambition, Mr. Garstin's withdrawal, and the daughters' marriages as the family's primary output.

memes.label: The Hospital
memes.description: The cholera hospital in Mei-tan-fu — a place of suffering and service where Walter Fane's scientific skill and the nuns' compassion converge in the struggle against the epidemic.

memes.label: The Laboratory
memes.description: Walter Fane's bacteriological laboratory in Mei-tan-fu — his sanctuary, his purpose, and the site of the scientific work that ultimately claims his life.

memes.label: The Law
memes.description: The Law in The Painted Veil represents the institutional framework of British authority — the profession that defines Mr. Garstin's life, the system that underpins colonial governance, and the moral structure against which individual desire and transgression are measured.

memes.label: The London Bar
memes.description: The legal profession in Edwardian London — the institution in which Mr. Garstin's career unfolded with quiet disappointment, and whose standards of success shaped Mrs. Garstin's social ambitions.

memes.label: The Edwardian Marriage Market
memes.description: The social institution of the London marriage market — the system of seasons, balls, and strategic introductions through which upper-middle-class women's lives were brokered, and the institutional context for Kitty's fateful choices.

memes.label: The Memorial Arch
memes.description: The richly carved memorial arch on the hill overlooking Mei-tan-fu, a "fantastic and ironical" landmark commemorating a virtuous widow that frames Kitty Fane's arrival and departure from the plague-stricken city.
aliases: the memorial to a virtuous widow

memes.label: The Mission School
memes.description: The school operated by the French nuns within the Mei-tan-fu convent — an educational institution for Chinese orphans that represents the transmission of knowledge and care across cultural boundaries.

memes.label: The Morning After
memes.description: The morning after Kitty's relapse with Townsend in Hong Kong — a moment of devastating self-recognition that precipitates her final break with the colonial world.

memes.label: The Painted Veil
memes.description: The 1925 novel by W. Somerset Maugham — a story of love, betrayal, and redemption set in colonial Hong Kong and cholera-stricken China, inspired by Dante's Purgatorio.

memes.label: The Peak
memes.description: The most prestigious residential district in colonial Hong Kong — home to the Governor and senior officials, its elevation physically mirrors the colony's social hierarchy.

memes.label: The River
memes.description: The river at Mei-tan-fu — a place of natural beauty that frames the novel's moral drama and provides Kitty with moments of contemplative peace amid the epidemic's horror.

memes.label: The Soul
memes.description: The soul in The Painted Veil represents the dimension of human experience that transcends social convention and physical desire — the locus of genuine moral growth, glimpsed through suffering, service, and the renunciation of vanity.

memes.label: The Street
memes.description: The streets in The Painted Veil — from the fashionable thoroughfares of London to the cholera-haunted lanes of Mei-tan-fu — serve as the novel's primary spaces of public exposure, where social performance and raw human suffering are most visible.

memes.label: The Verandah
memes.description: The verandah in The Painted Veil functions as a liminal architectural space — a threshold between the domestic interior and the colonial exterior — where crucial conversations, revelations, and moments of self-awareness occur.

memes.label: Townsend's Second Seduction
memes.description: Townsend's successful seduction of Kitty upon her return to Hong Kong after Walter's death — a relapse that fills Kitty with self-loathing and ultimately confirms her need to leave the colonial world permanently.

memes.label: Transformation
memes.description: "Kitty Fane's spiritual and moral transformation throughout The Painted Veil — from frivolous socialite to a woman of depth and compassion."

memes.label: Truth
memes.description: "The moral value of truth and truthfulness in The Painted Veil — a recurring tension between concealment and revelation, between social politeness and honest confrontation."

memes.label: Vanity
memes.description: The form of self-regard that Maugham identifies as the "painted veil" obscuring genuine human connection — manifested in Kitty's social ambitions, Townsend's self-satisfaction, and Walter's wounded pride.

memes.label: Waddington
memes.description: The Deputy Commissioner in Mei-tan-fu — a cynical, perceptive Englishman whose amused detachment and unexpected kindness make him the novel's most reliable observer of human nature.

memes.label: Waddington's House
memes.description: Waddington's residence in Mei-tan-fu — a modest house where Kitty finds friendship, honest conversation, and a glimpse of a life lived outside British colonial conventions.

memes.label: Walter Fane
memes.description: A government bacteriologist in colonial Hong Kong — intelligent, reserved, and emotionally complex, Walter Fane's quiet devotion and wounded pride drive the novel's central conflict.

memes.label: Walter's Cholera Work
memes.description: Walter Fane's bacteriological work during the Mei-tan-fu cholera epidemic — organizing sanitation, treating patients, and culturing the bacillus, the work that redeems his reputation and costs him his life.

memes.label: Walter's Death
memes.description: Walter Fane's death from cholera — the novel's pivotal event, simultaneously a martyrdom to science, a surrender to despair, and the liberation that enables Kitty's final transformation.

memes.label: Walter's Ultimatum
memes.description: Walter Fane's cold, methodical response to discovering Kitty's adultery — an ultimatum that forces the novel's central crisis and propels the characters toward Mei-tan-fu.

memes.label: W. Somerset Maugham
memes.description: "British playwright, novelist, and short story writer (1874–1965), author of The Painted Veil (1925)."
